coredata.dc:title: Desalination of mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate during the recycling of retired lithium ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Xiaoyan
Li, Xuan
Wei, Yanfang
Wu, Yixi
Yin, Jianguo
Yuan, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Generally, hydrometallurgy process is adopted in factory to recover valuable metal from anode materials in lithium ion battery. The mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate in this process is often managed by evaporative crystallization process which is high energy-consuming and expensive. The methodology employed in this research involves pretreatment and bipolar membrane electrodialysis to partition salts within the mother liquor into acids and bases, thereby achieving cost savings. The findings reveal that a significant 76.5 % reduction in COD is achievable through the application of potassium permanganate and activated carbon in the treatment of the mother liquor. Subsequently, the treated mother liquor can be transfer to electrodialysis process for further treatment. During this process, desalination of the mother liquor occurs, leading to the decomposition of sodium sulfate into sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide. Analysis of the experimental data reveals that optimizing the initial concentrations of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide within the acid and alkali collection chambers, along with the application of elevated electric current density and maintaining a high pH value of the mother liquor, yields improvements in current efficiency and concomitant reductions in energy consumption. Under optimal conditions, the current efficiency of acid and alkali preparation is 52.53 % and 62.12 % respectively, along with energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation is 8.68kWh/kg and 8.99kWh/kg respectively. The pretreatment and bipolar membrane electrodialysis process is low-energy consumption and inexpensive, facilitating a reduction in processing expenses to $6.659 per ton of mother liquor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium ion battery
Anode material
Mother liquor
Bipolar membrane electrodialysis
Desalination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Desalination of mother liquor by bipolar membrane electrodialysis
Effect of initial concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide
Effect of pH of the mother liquor
The operational costs of electrodialysis process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of bipolar membrane electrodialysis process (BP-bipolar membrane, A-anion exchange membrane, B-cation exchange membrane).

conductivity change of mother liquor and concentration change in acid and alkali chamber during electrodialysis process with different initial acid and alkali concentration（A-0.1 mol/L, B-0.20 mol/L, C-0.30 mol/L, D-0.40 mol/L）.

effect of initial concentration of acid and alkali on current efficiency and energy consumption（A-current efficiency and energy consumption of acid production, B-current efficiency and energy consumption of alkali production）.
conductivity change of mother liquor and concentration change in acid and alkali chamber during electrodialysis process with different electric current density（A-23.75 mA/cm2, B-11.88 mA/cm2）.
conductivity change of mother liquor and concentration change in acid and alkali chamber during electrodialysis process with different pH of mother liquor.
effect of pH of mother liquor on current efficiency and energy consumption.
Main composition of mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate.
the COD value in mother liquor after processed by different method (mg/L).

row.entry: Potassium Permanganate Oxidation followed by Activated Carbon Adsorption
Fenton Oxidation followed by Activated Carbon Adsorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: effect of electric current density on current efficiency and energy consumption.
operational costs and outputs of electrodialysis process.

head.ce:title: Desalination of mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate during the recycling of retired lithium ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Generally, hydrometallurgy process is adopted in factory to recover valuable metal from anode materials in lithium ion battery. The mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate in this process is often managed by evaporative crystallization process which is high energy-consuming and expensive. The methodology employed in this research involves pretreatment and bipolar membrane electrodialysis to partition salts within the mother liquor into acids and bases, thereby achieving cost savings. The findings reveal that a significant 76.5 % reduction in COD is achievable through the application of potassium permanganate and activated carbon in the treatment of the mother liquor. Subsequently, the treated mother liquor can be transfer to electrodialysis process for further treatment. During this process, desalination of the mother liquor occurs, leading to the decomposition of sodium sulfate into sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide. Analysis of the experimental data reveals that optimizing the initial concentrations of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide within the acid and alkali collection chambers, along with the application of elevated electric current density and maintaining a high pH value of the mother liquor, yields improvements in current efficiency and concomitant reductions in energy consumption. Under optimal conditions, the current efficiency of acid and alkali preparation is 52.53 % and 62.12 % respectively, along with energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation is 8.68kWh/kg and 8.99kWh/kg respectively. The pretreatment and bipolar membrane electrodialysis process is low-energy consumption and inexpensive, facilitating a reduction in processing expenses to $6.659 per ton of mother liquor.

ce:section.ce:para: Greenhouse gases discharged by fossil fuels combustion have profound impact on global climate which has aroused wide public concern [1]. The development of solar photovoltaic power, wind power and new energy vehicle are crucial for reducing the emission carbon dioxide, the leading greenhouse gas [2]. As an essential component, the lithium-ion battery is instrumental in supporting the storage needs of solar power generation, wind power generation and new energy vehicles [3,4]. Generally, lithium ion battery must be exchanged after about eight to ten years of service life [5]. The recycle of retired battery, especially the valuable metal in anode materials, is an effective method to prevent environmental pollution and resource scarcity [6–8]. Therefore, recycle of retired battery is crucial for the sustainable development of solar photovoltaic power, wind power and new energy vehicle. Currently, the processes containing leaching, purification, extraction separation and evaporative crystallization are employed in many factories to recover nickel sulfate, cobaltous sulfate, manganese sulfate and lithium carbonate from the anode of retired battery [9–11]. The most valuable lithium carbonate is precipitated from the raffinate by adding sodium carbonate: Li2SO4 +Na2CO3 = Li2CO3↓+Na2SO4. Therefore, a large amount of high salinity wastewater which is called mother liquor will be produced during the precipitation process. Owing to the rigorous regulatory stipulations prevalent in many regions regarding the release of high-salinity wastewater, the recourse to evaporative crystallization emerges as a common practice among a plethora of industrial facilities for wastewater treatment. Despite its achievement of zero emissions, this methodology is high energy-consuming and expensive. According to production data, the wastewater treatment costs reach up to $15.9–19.0 per ton with large amount of power consumption. Moreover, the by-product resultant from this process poses logistical challenges, given its inexpensive nature and constrained marketability, thereby mandating extensive warehousing infrastructure.

Bipolar membrane comprised of an anion-exchange membrane, cation-exchange membrane, and catalyst layer, exhibits dual functionality in anion and cation exchange. It can transfers salt into acid and alkali under external electric field [12,13]. Therefore, bipolar membrane electrodialysis can realize the resource utilization of high salinity wastewater with no chemical reagent addition. Yang [14] studied the recovery of acid and base from reverse osmosis by bipolar membrane electrodialysis. Under optimal conditions, the current efficiency and energy consumption of acid preparation was 67.7 % and 2.55kwh/kg respectively with the acid concentration reach up to 0.12 mol/L. Meanwhile, the current efficiency and energy consumption of alkali preparation was 87.5 % and 1.80kwh/kg respectively with the alkali concentration reach up to 0.18 mol/L. Gao [15] transferred sodium chloride into sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid by using bipolar membrane electrodialysis with the yield of sodium hydroxide reach up to 80.19 %. Wang [16] recovered sulfuric acid from vanadium leaching solution of shale by coupling diffusion dialysis and bipolar membrane electrodialysis. Experimental results showed that 71.12 % of free acid can be recycled with the pH of the leaching solution rose up to 1.8–2.5. The findings collectively suggest that bipolar membrane electrodialysis offers a viable avenue for harnessing the resource potential inherent in high-salinity wastewater.

Therefore, bipolar membrane electrodialysis was employed in this paper with the aim of converting sodium sulfate present in the mother liquor into sodium hydroxide and sulfuric acid. The generated sulfuric acid and desalted mother liquor could be returned to leaching process. The produced sodium hydroxide could be returned to purification process. Hence, achieving complete recycling of the mother liquor at a minimal cost is feasible.

All the chemical reagents employed were of analytical grade and de-ionized water was also used in the corresponding procedures during the experiments. The feed solution used in this study was obtained from Jiangxi Ruida new energy science and technology Co. Ltd and its composition was listed in Table 1. The primary constituent of the mother liquor is sodium sulfate, with a recorded pH level of 4.5. Besides, the mother liquor exhibits a chemical oxygen demand (COD) level of 640 mg/L, attributed possibly to lingering electrolytes in the anode materials and organics entrapped during the extraction separation procedures. Furthermore, the concentration of divalent metal ion in the mother liquor is lower than 5 mg/L such as Nickel, Cobalt and Manganese. That means divalent metal ion will not affect the electrodialysis process. Therefore, the mother liquor can be fed to bipolar membrane electrodialysis module after the organics is removed.

The electrodialysis system (EX-3BT), bipolar membrane (BP-2), anion-exchange membrane (AHT) and cation-exchange membrane (CT-4) are all obtained from Lanran Science and Technology Ltd. In the electrodialysis system, the bipolar membrane, anion-exchange membrane, and cation-exchange membrane are arranged in alternating sequence, as illustrated in Fig. 1 depicting the operational principles. The mother liquor is fed into the desalting chamber when its organics are removed. One side of the desalting chamber is anion-exchange membrane which is close to anode. The sulfate ion exhibits permeability through the anion-exchange membrane, while sodion is selectively rejected. Positioned adjacent to the cathode, the opposite side of the desalting chamber features the cation-exchange membrane. The sodion can pass the cation-exchange membrane and the sulfate ion is refused. Therefore, the sulfate ions in the desalting chamber move towards anode and pass the anion-exchange membrane under the action of electric field. Then, sulfate ions migrate to the acid chamber where they encounter hydrogen ions released from the bipolar membrane electrodialysis process, resulting in the accumulation of sulfuric acid within the acid chamber. Similarly, sodium hydroxide will be collected in alkali chamber. Once the majority of sodium and sulfate ions have migrated into the alkali and acid chambers, respectively, the mother liquor undergoes desalination. The desalinated mother liquor, along with the sulfuric acid, can be reintroduced into the leaching process, while the sodium hydroxide can either be redirected to the purification process or marketed for sale. This approach ensures optimal resource utilization of the mother liquor.

The electrodialysis experiment was carried out according to the following procedure in EX-3BT. Firstly, the tanks for electrodialysis system were filled with different solution: the desalting chamber, acid chamber and alkali chamber were filled with mother liquor, dilute sulphuric acid and dilute sodium hydroxide respectively. In addition, the solution volume for different tank was all set to be 500 ml. The anode and cathode liquor which is sodium sulfate solution was also fed into a separated tank. Secondly, the circulating pump was started to allow the solution recycling between the tank and chamber. After 20 min of circulation, the electrodialysis experiment was carried out by applying voltage across the membrane. During the electrodialysis experiment, operational data were logged at 20 min intervals. This data encompassed membrane current, electrical conductivity, and solution pH within the desalting chamber, as well as the solution volumes within the acid, alkali, and desalting chambers. Additionally, the concentrations of sulfuric acid in the acid chamber and sodium hydroxide in the alkali chamber were analyzed every 20 min. Finally, the electrodialysis experiment will be finished when the electrical conductivity of solution in desalting chamber decreased to near 0 mS/cm. The effect of electric current density, pH of mother liquor, initial concentration of sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide on current efficiency and energy consumption will be studied.

The energy consumption (EC) of sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide preparation during the electrodialysis can be calculated as follow:
(1)
EC =
∫
UIdt
(
C
t
V
t
−
C
0
V
0
) M

In Eq. (1), U and I are the voltage (V) and electric current (A) across the membrane, respectively; t is the running time (S) of the electrodialysis experiment; C
t and V
t are the concentration (mol/L) and volume (L) of the solution in acid or alkali chamber at the time t, respectively; C
0 and V
0 are the initial concentration (mol/L) and volume (L) of the solution in acid or alkali chamber, respectively; M is the molar mass of sulphuric acid (98 g/mol) or sodium hydroxide (40 g/mol).

The current efficiency (CE) of sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide preparation during the electrodialysis can be calculated as follow:
(2)
CE =
Z (
C
t
V
t
−
C
0
V
0
) F
NI t

In Eq. (2), Z is the valence of sulfate ion (2) or sodion (1); F is the faraday constant and its value is 96485 C/mol; N is the membrane couples of the electrodialysis equipment and its value is 3 in this study.

In order to obtain accurate energy consumption and current efficiency of sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide preparation during the electrodialysis, the data after the conductivity of mother liquor reduced to zero was not used to calculate energy consumption and current efficiency.

Acid and alkali concentrations in the acid chamber and alkali chamber were determined utilizing the acid-alkali neutralization titrimetric method. Titration of sulfuric acid in the acid chamber employed methyl red as an indicator, while titration of sodium hydroxide in the alkali chamber utilized phenolphthalein. The pH and electrical conductivity of solution in desalting chamber were detected by pH Meter and conductivity meter, respectively. The COD in mother liquor was analyzed by potassium permanganate oxidation method. The concentration of metal ions in the mother liquor was quantified using atomic absorption spectrometry.

The COD in mother liquor reaches up to 640 mg/L according to Table 1. This may attributed to the residual electrolyte in anode materials and the entrained organics in extraction separation process. The organics in mother liquor may foul the membrane, which will lead to decrease of current efficiency and increase of energy consumption. Therefore, the organics in mother liquor must be removed before the electrodialysis experiment. In industrial processes, various methodologies such as Potassium Permanganate Oxidation, Fenton Oxidation and Ozonation, can be used to remove the organics in the solution. Fenton Oxidation and Potassium Permanganate Oxidation followed by Activated Carbon Adsorption were used in this paper to decrease the COD in the mother liquor and the results are showed in Table 2.

Table 2 shows that the COD removal ratio reaches up to 76.5 % when the mother liquor was oxidized by Potassium Permanganate and processed by Activated Carbon. Additionally, the COD value can be decreased from 640 mg/L to 150.5 mg/L, achieving compliance with the requirement of electrodialysis experiment. Upon implementation of Fenton Oxidation coupled with Activated Carbon Adsorption, the COD concentration in the mother liquor decreased from 640 mg/L to 451.1 mg/L, yielding a removal efficiency of merely 29.5 %. Therefore, the process containing Potassium Permanganate Oxidation and Activated Carbon Adsorption is considered to be an effective method for the removal of organics in mother liquor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desalination of mother liquor by bipolar membrane electrodialysis
ce:section.ce:para: The pretreated mother liquor was used as feeding material for bipolar membrane electrodialysis. Then, the effect of initial concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide, electric current density and pH value of mother liquor on current efficiency and energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation were studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide
ce:section.ce:para:
Dilute sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide with different concentration was fed into acid chamber and alkali chamber respectively. Subsequently, the conductivity change of mother liquor and concentration change of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide was presented in Fig. 2. It showed that the conductivity of mother liquor decreased as the electrodialysis time increased, while the concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide increased gradually. The decrease of conductivity indicates the decrease of sodium sulfate concentration in mother liquor, which was due to the decomposition of sodium sulfate during the electrodialysis process:
Na2SO4+2 H2O=2NaOH+H2SO4

The generated sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide diffused into acid chamber and alkali chamber respectively under electric field, which led to the increase of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide. It can also be seen from Fig. 2 that the increment of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide were not equal. The sulfuric acid concentration was always lower than sodium hydroxide concentration. This may be attributed to the reverse migration of sulfuric acid under concentration field.

The current efficiency and energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation were calculated according to the data in Fig. 2 and the results were showed in Fig. 3. As can be seen from Fig. 3, the energy consumption of sodium hydroxide preparation increased from 9.08kWh/kg to 11.11kWh/kg when the initial acid and alkali concentration increased from 0.10 mol/L to 0.40 mol/L, while the current efficiency decreased from 57.57 % to 46.99 %. Simultaneously, we can also draw a conclusion from Fig. 3 that the high initial concentration of acid and alkali do not appreciably enhance the ultimate concentrations of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide. During the electrodialysis process, high initial acid and alkali concentration in acid chamber and alkali chamber will lift the resistance of ionic transportation, which will increase the energy consumption and decrease the current efficiency [17]. In fact, the purpose of adding dilute sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide in acid chamber and alkali chamber was to improve the electrical conductivity at the beginning of electrodialysis. Therefore, 0.1 mol/L was considered as appropriate initial concentration of acid and alkali.

The effect of electric current density on current efficiency and energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation was tested when the initial electric current density was 23.75 mA/cm2 and 11.88 mA/cm2. Fig. 4 shows the conductivity change of mother liquor and concentration change of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide. It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the conductivity of the mother liquor was decreased to 0 when the electrolysis was carried out and continued for about 80 min with the electric current density of 23.75 mA/cm2, while the electrolysis time increased to 220 min with the electric current density of 11.88 mA/cm2. This indicated that the decomposition rate of sodium sulfate increased with the rising of electric current density. Similarly, the concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide increased gradually when the electrolysis time extended. Notably, the sulfuric acid concentration consistently remained subordinate to that of sodium hydroxide, a consequence of sulfuric acid's reverse migration in the presence of the concentration gradient.

The current efficiency and energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation at different electric current density were calculated based on the data provided in Fig. 4, with the results summarized in Table 3. As can be seen from Table 3, the current efficiency of acid and alkali preparation was decreasing by 21.73 % and 15.75 % respectively when the electric current density decreased from 23.75 mA/cm2 to 11.88 mA/cm2. Meanwhile, the energy consumption of acid preparation was increasing by 1.54kWh/kg when the electric current density decreased from 23.75 mA/cm2 to 11.88 mA/cm2. While the energy consumption of alkali preparation was nearly the same. This may be attributed to the reverse migration of sulfuric acid increased at long electrolysis time when low electric current density was executed. Consequently, high electric current density will be benefit for the desalination of mother liquor by using bipolar membrane electrodialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of the mother liquor
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the mother liquor may affect the efficiency of the electrodialysis process. Thus, the electrodialysis experiments were conducted after adjusting the pH of the mother liquor to 4, 6, and 9 by using sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide solution. The alterations in conductivity of the mother liquor and the variations in concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide were recorded and depicted in Fig. 5. Subsequently, the current efficiency and energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation at different pH were calculated and presented in Fig. 6. We can conclude from Fig. 5 that the conductivity of mother liquor was decreased to zero when the electrolysis was continued to 140 min ignoring the pH change of the mother liquor. Moreover, the concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide was increased to 0.7 mol/L and 0.8 mol/L respectively. Accordingly, variations in pH levels within the mother liquor have negligible effects on changes in its conductivity and the concentrations of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide.

However, the pH of mother liquor obviously affects the current efficiency and energy consumption of the electrodialysis process. As can be seen from Fig. 6, the current efficiency of alkali preparation increased from 53.96 % to 62.12 % and the energy consumption of alkali preparation decreased from 10.35kWh/kg to 8.99kWh/kg when the pH of the mother liquor increased from 4 to 9. Meanwhile, the current efficiency of acid preparation increased from 42.43 % to 52.53 % and the energy consumption of acid preparation decreased from 10.75kWh/kg to 8.68kWh/kg. The enhancement of current efficiency and reduction in energy consumption were observed with an increase in the pH of the mother liquor, potentially due to the migration of H+ during the electrodialysis process. The charge of H+ is equal to Na+, and, H+ has small radius than Na+. As a result, the migration rate of H+ surpasses that of Na+. Although OH- has a smaller radius compared to SO4 2-, their charge is lower, potentially resulting in a slower migration rate for OH- compared to SO4 2-. Therefore, the H+ in mother liquor will pass through the anionic membrane to decrease the alkali concentration in alkali chamber, which will decrease the current efficiency and increase the energy consumption. The migration of H+ will decrease when increase the pH of the mother liquor increase. Hence, a higher pH level within the mother liquor conferred advantages to the electrodialysis process. By factoring in the initial pH of the mother liquor, adjustments can be made to maintain a pH of 9 throughout the electrodialysis procedure. In addition, the final pH of the mother liquor was lower than the initial pH and the current efficiency of acid preparation was lower than the current efficiency of alkali preparation. This was also attributed to the reverse migration of sulfuric acid under concentration field, which can also be verified in section of 3.2.1 and 3.2.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The operational costs of electrodialysis process
ce:section.ce:para: In the scope of this study, the operational costs of desalination of mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate by electrodialysis process were estimated. To simplify the calculation, the manpower costs were not taken into consideration and only the costs of chemicals and energy consumption were included in the calculation. The consumption, outputs and market prices of the chemicals and energy are listed in Table 4.

As shown in Table 4, the highest costs come from the consumption of energy, and the energy consumption reaches up to $20.490 per cubic meter of wastewater. Regarding chemicals consumption, the KMnO4 and Activated carbon consumption are $1.726 and $0.620 respectively per cubic meter of wastewater, which are insignificant when they were compared with the energy consumption. It should be noted that 31.68 kg sulfuric acid and 30.14 kg sodium hydroxide will be produced when 1 cubic meter of wastewater is treated by electrodialysis process. This extra benefit reaches up to $16.177 per cubic meter of wastewater. Therefore, the operational costs of the electrodialysis process can be calculated by subtracting the benefits and the result is also showed in Table 4. It can be concluded from Table 4 that the operational costs of electrodialysis process is $6.659 per cubic meter of mother liquor. According to the production data, the operational costs of evaporation process is $15.9–19.0 per ton of mother liquor. Thus, at least 58.12 % of the operational costs will be saved when evaporation process is replaced by electrodialysis process.

Pretreatment and electrodialysis process were applied to manage the mother liquor generated from the precipitation of lithium carbonate during the recycling of retired lithium ion battery. Experimental findings elucidated the desalination of mother liquor and preparation of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide were realized during the electrodialysis process. The COD value can be decreased from 640 mg/L to 150.5 mg/L when the mother liquor was oxidized by potassium permanganate and treated by activated carbon. The removal ratio of COD reaches up to 76.5 % and the treated mother liquor was suit for electrodialytic treatment. During the electrodialysis process, the current efficiency and energy consumption of alkali and acid preparation are dependent on the initial concentrations of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide, electric current density, and the pH of the mother liquor. Low initial concentration of sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide, high electric current density and pH of the mother liquor were benefit for improving current efficiency and reducing energy consumption. Under optimized parameters, the current efficiency of acid and alkali preparation were 52.53 % and 62.12 % respectively, the energy consumption of acid and alkali preparation were 8.68kWh/kg and 8.99kWh/kg respectively. The operational cost associated with the electrodialysis process for desalinating mother liquor amounted to $6.659 per cubic meter. Substituting the evaporation process with electrodialysis can yield a minimum savings of 58.12 % in operational expenses.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51604055), the “China Scholarship Council” fellowship (CSC201802075009) and the Chongqing Science and Technology Commission in China (No. CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0399).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recovery of cobalt and lithium from spent lithium ion batteries using organic citric acid as leachant
J HAZARD MATER

Wang, Research on Separation and Recovery of Sulfuric Acid from Vanadium-containing Shale Acid Leaching Solution by Diffusion Dialysis Coupling Bipolar Membrane Electrodialysis, Master's degree, Wuhan: Wuhan University of Science and Technology, 2018 (in Chinese).
